sections.content:
An **AI model** is a software system trained on large amounts of data to perform tasks like answering questions, writing, coding, or analysing information.

When people talk about 'AI' today, they're usually referring to **large language models (LLMs)** — models trained specifically on text. These include Claude, ChatGPT, Gemini, Llama, and others.

Every model is built by a company or research lab. They differ in:
- **Training data** — what they learned from
- **Size** — how many parameters (roughly: how 'smart' they are at different tasks)
- **Specialisation** — some are better at code, others at reasoning or conversation
- **Pricing** — cost per token (chunk of text processed)
- **Availability** — API access, web interface, or both

The model you use matters. The same prompt can give meaningfully different results depending on which model you send it to.

sections.bad: Just use ChatGPT for everything — it's all the same under the hood.
sections.good: Different models have different strengths. Claude tends to excel at long documents and nuanced writing. GPT-4o is strong at general tasks and image understanding. Gemini integrates with Google's ecosystem. Choose based on the task.
sections.explanation: Treating all AI models as interchangeable means you'll often use the wrong tool. Understanding the landscape lets you route tasks to the best model — which can mean better results and lower costs.

sections.prompt: A colleague says 'all AI chatbots are basically the same thing.' What's the most accurate response?

choices: They're right — all LLMs produce identical outputs for the same prompt
They differ in training data, strengths, pricing, and ideal use cases
They're the same technology but different interfaces
Only open-source models are different; commercial ones are identical
data.explanation: Models differ substantially in what they're good at, how they handle complex tasks, their context window size, pricing, and more. Knowing these differences helps you make better choices.

sections.prompt: You need to process 200 pages of legal documents for a summary. Which factor matters most when choosing a model?

choices: Which one has the nicest interface
Which one has the largest context window
Which one was released most recently
Which one your company uses for email
data.explanation: Context window size determines how much text a model can process at once. For long documents, a model with a large context window (e.g. 200k tokens) avoids the need to chunk and re-send the document, preserving coherence.
